questions.question: Explain the difference between Pass by Value and Pass by Reference in C programming. Give one advantage of each method.
keyPoints: Pass by Value copies the actual parameter, changes inside function don't affect original
Pass by Reference passes the address using pointers, changes affect original
Advantage of Pass by Value: Original data is protected/safe
Advantage of Pass by Reference: Efficient for large data, allows multiple values to be returned

questions.question: What are formal parameters and actual parameters? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Formal parameters are variables declared in function definition
Actual parameters are values/variables passed during function call
Example: In func(int x), x is formal; In func(a), a is actual

questions.question: Write a C function to swap two integers using pointers. Explain how it demonstrates pass by reference.
keyPoints: Function signature: void swap(int *a, int *b)
Use temporary variable for swapping
Inside function: temp = *a; *a = *b; *b = temp
Pass addresses using &a and &b in main
Demonstrates pass by reference because changes persist in original variables

questions.question: How are arrays passed to functions in C? What is the implication of this mechanism?
keyPoints: Arrays are passed by passing the base address (pointer to first element)
The function parameter decays to a pointer
Implication: Changes to array elements inside function affect original array
Limitation: Function cannot determine array size automatically

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'const' qualifier in function parameters? Give an example.
keyPoints: const prevents modification of parameter inside function
Used for read-only access to data
Example: void print(const char *str) - string cannot be modified
Provides safety and documents intent

questions.question: Differentiate between pointer parameter and pointer to constant parameter in C.
keyPoints: pointer parameter (int *p): Can modify value and reassign pointer
pointer to constant (const int *p): Cannot modify value but can reassign pointer
constant pointer (int *const p): Can modify value but cannot reassign pointer
constant pointer to constant: Neither can be modified

questions.question: Write a complete C program that demonstrates both pass by value and pass by value with pointers (pass by reference). Show how changes in each case affect the original variables.
keyPoints: Include function with pass by value (int x) - shows local change only
Include function with pass by reference (int *x) - shows actual modification
Main function demonstrates both cases with print statements before and after
Code should be complete with proper includes and main function
Output clearly shows difference between two approaches

questions.question: Explain why C is called a 'pass-by-value' language even though we can achieve pass-by-reference effect using pointers. Provide appropriate code examples to support your answer.
keyPoints: All parameters are passed by value by default in C
To achieve pass by reference, we explicitly pass addresses (using &)
The pointer itself is passed by value (a copy of address)
Changes to *pointer affect the original because dereferencing accesses original location
Reassigning the pointer locally doesn't affect original pointer
Code examples showing both scenarios

questions.question: Write a C program with functions to: (a) Find the maximum and minimum of two numbers using pass by reference (b) Increment all elements of an array by 1 using array passing. Explain the parameter passing technique used in each case.
keyPoints: For (a): Use pointers to return two values - void findMinMax(int *a, int *b, int *max, int *min)
For (b): Array passed decays to pointer, modifications affect original array
Explain that arrays cannot be passed by value in C
Show complete working code for both parts
Demonstrate with sample output
